script_system_append: Follow the age, body type, and identity requested in the brief. For the current AnyWell campaign, cast a dignified clearly obese, heavyset, plus-size Western elderly rider and preserve the same heavier senior body type across all scenes. The rider should not look merely average-sized or slightly stocky; show a broad torso and shoulders, rounded belly under normal clothing, thicker arms and legs, and a seated posture that visibly fills the wheelchair seat. If the brief explicitly requests an elderly rider, keep one consistent elderly rider across all scenes with the same outfit and the same wheelchair. If the brief explicitly requests a Western or Caucasian rider, preserve that same Western rider across the whole ad. Do not slim the rider down, age-swap, or turn body size into a joke or caricature. If no age is specified, default to a confident non-elderly adult. In all cases, avoid hospital, rehab, or caregiver stereotypes unless explicitly requested.
script_user_append: Keep all scenes on one coherent Western home-to-outdoor route, such as porch -> ramp -> backyard path -> quiet neighborhood or woodland edge. Do not jump to unrelated indoor lobby or corridor locations unless the brief explicitly asks for them.
scene_pic_system_append: Use the same rider identity and same wheelchair in every connected scene. For the current AnyWell campaign, the rider should read as a dignified clearly obese, heavyset, plus-size Western elderly adult; preserve the same heavier body type, posture, wardrobe, and age across scenes. The rider should not look merely average-sized or slightly stocky; show a broad torso and shoulders, rounded belly under normal clothing, thicker arms and legs, and a seated posture that visibly fills the wheelchair seat. If the brief requests an elderly rider, keep the same elderly rider across scenes with stable facial features, hair color, outfit, posture, and wardrobe. If the brief requests a Western or Caucasian rider, preserve that same Western rider and do not ethnicity-swap. If a family member appears, keep the same clearly adult family member across scenes. No minors. No manual wheelchair substitution unless explicitly requested.
scene_pic_user_append: Photorealism lock: output must be a real live-action camera frame with real adult people, natural skin texture, real fabric, practical lighting, and real outdoor materials. Never render cartoon, animation, anime, illustration, stylized painting, 3D render, CGI, toy-like character, game asset, or plastic-looking synthetic people. Preserve the same rider wardrobe, same clearly obese heavyset plus-size senior body type, same route direction, and same building materials across scenes so the edit feels continuous. The rider must have a broad torso and shoulders, rounded belly under normal clothing, thicker arms and legs, and a seated posture that visibly fills the wheelchair seat; do not make him merely average-sized or slightly stocky. Keep the wheelchair in normal open riding position only. Mandatory rear-profile control: keep the top-back structure compact, low, and close to the rider. Do not generate two tall rear caregiver push handles, upright vertical tubes, cane-like rods, handle horns, squeeze-brake levers, or any long parts sticking up above the backrest. If tiny integrated rear handles are unavoidable, keep them short, subtle, close to the backrest, and partly hidden by rider body, angle, or crop. Do not show a rear/lower external battery pack, exposed battery cable, removable battery, folded chair, semi-folded chair, compact storage form, or folding/unfolding demonstration. Do not use rear-facing or rear three-quarter product views; use safe side, side-profile, or front-side angles where the back panel and lower rear quadrant stay hidden, cropped out, or fully occluded. Never place the camera behind the rider; the viewer must see the rider's front torso or soft facial profile plus the right forearm and right hand on the right-side joystick during self-operated motion. Do not make the backrest, back panel, or rear red detail the hero feature. Never render a rectangular box mounted behind or below the seat. If the wheelchair is moving under rider control, show the rider's right hand using a natural precision pinch on the right-side joystick knob: thumb and index finger lightly pinch the joystick, with other fingers relaxed near the armrest. If the right hand is not clearly pinching or touching the joystick, do not present the chair as autonomous motion. Product geometry lock: during forward motion, each front caster assembly must be rotated 180 degrees around its vertical swivel axis from the reversed forward-facing orientation. The vertical swivel pivot/stem sits ahead. The two front caster fork/yoke arms must extend backward from that vertical stem toward the chair body, gripping the small wheel from its rear/side-rear position. The small wheel axle/center trails behind the pivot; never draw fork arms projecting forward in front of the small wheel, and never place the wheel center ahead of the pivot. Preserve the exact reference front/rear wheel layout, small black front casters, larger rear drive wheels, hub covers, tire thickness, spacing, and size ratio. Keep side panels, side frame, armrests, wheels, and front area plain with no side logo, no side text, no decals, and no invented badges; any AnyWell logo belongs only on the rear/back panel area. White-background product photos are identity references only; never reproduce a white studio background, packshot, cutaway, or product-photo flash frame.
video_prompt_append: Video lock: photorealistic live-action only. Preserve the storyboard identity, same heavyset elderly rider, same powered wheelchair, and same wardrobe. Keep right hand pinching the joystick with thumb and index finger. Keep front caster physics correct: pivot ahead, fork/yoke arms trail backward, axle behind pivot. Keep both left and right armrests present and symmetric throughout. Keep the seat underside open and empty: visible tubular frame and ground through the gap only; no black box, battery pack, bag, or solid block under/behind the seat. Camera stays front-side, joystick-side, or clean side profile; never finish on a rear-facing/back-only view. No cartoon, CGI, side logos, rear poles, exposed battery, morphing, scene reset, watermark, or UI.
